title: The Lies of Locke Lamora
author: Scott Lynch
review: everything good about good fantasy! and done sooo well. all aspects of fantasy was so well balanced. never a dull moment. even though, on principle, i generally don’t start series that’re on-hiatus, everyone (esp. my girlfriend) who told me this book is worth breaking my rule for was absolutely right (she always is)! what a master piece. i mean, stories, good stories, about found family always gets me anyways, but this one was done so well, my heart aches. i highly recommend to anyone who enjoys funny, clever, fun time reads that also breaks your heart; but if you’re a sucker for found-family-tropes as i am, then YOU MUST READ THIS RIGHT THIS INSTANT TYVM ok byeee

title: Blood Over Bright Haven
author: M.L. Wang
review: I STAYED UP ALL NIGHT AND ABSOLUTELY DEVOURED THIS BOOK. sleep? gone. work? rip. but heart full of feels and head full of thoughts. favourite book this year? favourite book in a long time? or is this just a really good fantasy book after months of not reading any fantasy; just a fantasy boy starved for fantasy? idk? but what i do know is, i don’t remember ever actually almost crying in the first literal chapter of a book (and chapter 3 actually made me cry; but for a different reason). absolutely masterful writing. right before i started this book yesterday, my girlfriend told me that she hasn’t stopped thinking of this book since she read it more than a year ago; and i get it now. i don’t think i’m gonna stop thinking about this book for a long time as well. this standalone book achieve so much in so few pages as many epic series fail to ... ugh.

title: The Death of Ivan Ilych
author: Leo Tolstoy
review: it was my first time reading tolstoy, and i'm glad i did not read this earlier in my life. i wouldn't have appreciated it nearly as much (or maybe my takeaways would've just been wildly different). this book is about the introspective hindsight on a conventional life as this man is dying. maybe i should reread this a decade later, just as a sanity check.

title: Seven Brief Lessons on Physics
author: Carlo Rovelli
review: perfectly as advertised on the literal first line in the book: lessons written for those who know little or nothing about modern science. i can't see how it could've done any better. the later chapters will be (and were, for me) informative even if you've formally studied physics past high school, but they are still written in a super accessible manner. highly recommend to pretty much anybody.

title: Reckoning
author: Michael R.  Miller
review: a truly, truly satisfying experience. it was everything i hoped this series would progress to be and more! can't wait for the last and final book.

title: Platform Decay
author: Martha Wells
review: if something like this plot had happened any earlier in the series, i might’ve thought, "meh, just another aw-grumpy-bots-human-constructs-is-a-big-softie-when-kids-are-in-danger story". but now, after all this character development, it felt so much more believable and realistic. i’ll repeat what i said in an earlier review: it’s weird how much one (i) can relate to murderbot, it's almost like that’s the whole point martha wells wanted to make. thank you very much. can't wait for more.

title: A Parade of Horribles
author: Matt Dinniman
review: absolutely worth the wait. tried savouring it for a hot minute but failed miserably after chapter 38, and absolutely feral binged it. chapter 38 was a bit of a game changer if i may say so. but also, chapter 57 might be the greatest piece of fiction i’ve ever read, and it's for sure the best x funniest piece of fiction i've ever heard narrated (jeff is a genuis). matt is also an absolute genius (of course). just as funny, or probably funnier than i remember. and as always, what an ending. crrrrazy levels of plot progression. i loved it soooo much aaaahhhh. now i have to wait forever for the next one aaaaaaaahhhhhhhh.

title: System Collapse
author: Martha Wells
review: martha wells is a genius. this book is a masterpiece. but more importantly this book is a masterclass; if i ever wanna write truly intelligent characters solving hard problems, while being endlessly witty and insightful, i'd ask myself what martha wells would make murderbot do. 10 outta 10 would recommend. :')

title: Fugitive Telemetry
author: Martha Wells
review: 3.5/5! loved the story, funny and engaging and witty as always, but it felt more like book #5.5 instead of book #6. i want more! i hope the next book delivers! [i am aware it's on me for expecting a full novel from a novella]

title: Exit Strategy
author: Martha Wells
review: 5 stars! i loved it! i mean, i loved how funny, emotional, character driven the first three books were as well but this felt like a step up still. it's ending also felt like the natural conclusion (or atleast plot resolution) of the major plot line started in book one. and yes, i binged it. i'm probably gonna take a break before the next book (the first full novel in the series). i'm gonna save it for the next time i want a funny, heart-string-tugging, crazy fun, fun time. such a fun idea. such beautiful execution. and an overall brilliant time! if you have 2-3 hours to spare, i'd highly recommend all four novellas so far! c'mon!

title: Network Effect
author: Martha Wells
review: this is exactly what i wanted from a full length murderbot book! beautiful character moments, still funny as always. the tension, the resolutions, the characters, i loved it all. one of my favorite character duos as well. i hope i get more of them in the future.

title: Rogue Protocol
author: Martha Wells
review: three books in and i'm hooked hooked! ever since book one, this bot has been brutally relatable; wants to avoid humans, socially anxious and prefers to rot watching media all day long. it's a novella, so it's not fair to expect more world building or plot given how strong the character work is, but this book had me one-more-chapter-one-more-chapter-ing all the way to the end of the book and absolutely demolishing my sleep cycle. worth it. can't wait to get to the novel. and each novella so far, i'm increasing being attached to and moved by the other new bots we meet. i'm scared for what's to come. but i'm looking forward to the major plot line to progress.

title: Artificial Condition
author: Martha Wells
review: super fun, maybe not as fun as the first book, but i loved the new characters we met, and i still dislike how little of them we get to see before the pages run out. gonna binge atleast one more of these this weekend.

title: White Nights
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
review:
well, whoops :)))))
i get it now. i thought dostoevsky was for stuffy eng lit majors, and boy was i wrong. bring back yearning. super accessible, and relatable (painfully so), and raw; especially for something written more than a century and a half ago.
it joins my to-re-read every few years pile.

title: All Systems Red
author: Martha Wells
review: what a fun little read! maybe, just maybe sci-fi books are super fun and i've been missing out, oops. it ended way too quickly ... murderbot diaries binge incoming, i just know it.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
review: 5/5 stars. i’m a mess. for years i dismissed this as just some random sci-fi space story but i was so wrong. my friends and i always talk about how starved we are for good friendship in fiction. who’d have thought i’d find one of the most fulfilling bond ever in a space book?

title: Mother Mary Comes to Me
author: Arundhati Roy
review: this is possibly the best book i've read in a long long time. i haven't felt an urge to re-read a book as soon as i finished reading it as this one.. such eloquent prose. super insightful. deeply moving. highly recommend.

title: A War of Wyverns
author: S.F. Williamson
review: a perfect 3.5/5! it climbed 0.5 when i picked it back up from 70% yesterday. the first half dragged a lot. and it really feels like a story that has gone past where it should've ended beautifully. don't get me wrong, the prose is good, and the linguistics is super fun, the world is super interesting, the dragon+human vs dragon+human action is probably one of the best (can actually visualise) i've read, but the plot, by the end of this book should've ended imo. i might come back if the reviews for the next book is amazing. otherwise this is it for me, with this series. loved the first book though.

title: Words of Radiance
author: Brandon Sanderson
review: edit: now that i've read oathbringer, words of radiance was just happy days, such happy days, i miss you

title: A Language of Dragons
author: S.F. Williamson
review: what a perfect book to binge to kick off my 2026 reading year. i loved it. 4.5/5. picked up the book because i loved the premise: dragons, linguistics, political intrigue, academia, historical fiction-esque rebellion and it delivered above and beyond. loved the characters, loved the narrative. it is a YA book, and it's YA done right. it's super accessible for people who haven't read the hunger games, babel, or other similar books. and even if you're an avid fantasy reader, it feels good to read easier, accessible writing that gets the point across while still being enjoyable prose. would especially recommend to linguistics/linguist-fiction enjoyers.

title: Fahrenheit 451
author: Ray Bradbury
review: crazy how this was written before even TVs were mainstream, yet it continues to be relevant

title: Into the Wild
author: Jon Krakauer
review: [3.5/5] it's always interesting reading books after watching it's film adaptation, so most of the beginning felt like a recap. it took me a while but i enjoyed jon krakauer's commentary and especially enjoyed his memoir-y chapters. but by the end of the book, i was reminded of why and how much the movie moved me in my youth.

title: Jade Legacy
author: Fonda Lee
review: [4.5/5] what a series. the time skips in this book is crazy. it goes through years and years, so it's almost like reading a history text, but each new chapter you know something BIG is gonna happen because the time skipped to this point for something important. wow. but for something paced like a history text, the action and the emotional beats are so intense! and literally everything feels like a coin toss; i don't think anything was trope-y. the literally-anything-can-happen-ness of it makes it read even more like history imo. in this story there are ONLY morally grey characters. there are no good guys. but man i think hilo is for sure a fav character i will think about forever. paraphrasing from the book “i love him, and sometimes i think i hate him. i’m nothing like him and don’t want to be. yet somehow all i want is to live up to him.” ALSO sooo many banger one liners, like – “It’s obvious you’re not a father, Lieutenant [...] Or you wouldn’t feel so invincible”!

title: Hamnet
author: Maggie O'Farrell
review: rip my heart. i literally cried my eyes out in part two tyvm. and such lyrical, visceral prose. anges. i won't say anything more. go read it.

title: Perfection
author: Vincenzo Latronico
review: ey yoo idk if reading this made me feel personally attacked or deeply seen or both. definitely re-reading. if you're a millennial or an expat, i highly recommend.

title: Anxious People
author: Fredrik Backman
review: ok, temme why this was superrr addicting?! what?! thought i’d read few chapters of a lit fic and sleep, y’know. but no. sleep gonnn. sleep cycle ruined. I don’t think any other author, but frederick backman makes me read through teary eyes, while i’m somehow sobbing and laughing at the same time. what a genius. what a truly thoroughly <i>enjoyable<i> read.

title: The Book That Held Her Heart
author: Mark  Lawrence
review:
i am speechless. 11/10. 6 stars. i absolutely loved it. what a beautiful series. it hurt, but it was so beautiful and so worth it. ugh, so beautiful i cry. before i go into spoiler rambling you should know how legendary the references, shoutouts, call-outs, and the logic/execution of the space-time-chance-world-exchange was. wow.


WHEN GAMDOT SHOWED UP WITH A WIDE BRIMMED HAT I DIEDD, I DIEEDDDD. and DUDE duude the anne frank storyline was literally literary perfection wtf mark lawrence. absolute genius. almost as genius as the space-time-chance-world-exchange logics. it feels almost cliched (as a concept) referencing anne frank in a plot-line about burning books, but the past two books in this series set it up so beautifully, so perfectly, ughhhh. it had it to. the execution was legendary. and the epigraphs continue to be an absolute menace huehuehue loved it! and boy did i miss carlotte in the previous book. phew. this book felt like a 6-star just a few chapters in onwards. and it just continued getting better and better. i love yute. i loved the ending. “Hey, sleepyhead. What shall we do today?”


everybody who loves reading, and loved books should read this at some point imo and you should check out this truly poetic, short, banger of a review for this banger of a book here.

title: The Book That Broke the World
author: Mark  Lawrence
review:
i- wha- eh- what even?! criminal cliff hanger! my heart goes out to everybody who had to wait a whole year (almost) to read the next book ... truly criminal cliff hanger ... but ooooh boy was it all worth it. it's even better than the first book, in even fewer pages. gonna go right to the next book, i'm scared..

also, i’ve never appreciated, much less enjoyed, the epigraphs in any other books the way i have in this series so far!

title: The Book That Wouldn’t Burn
author: Mark  Lawrence
review:
the way i devoured this book. it's been a while since i literally stayed up way too late to my general detriment reading a book. 10/10 worth it. what a beautiful, unique read. no idea how it managed to feel unique and yet so intimately familiar?? how?? my friend N said it best imo, "there’s people who love books and want to tell a story, and then there’s people who love books and even in their stories, that love bleeds through." and it truly bleeds through! it reminded me of piranasi, and being a stubborn child (barely a fraction as cerebral as the main character, livira). and i love the characters! all of them! every single named character is interesting, and that single fact is endlessly interesting imo! go read this book!

p.s. i debated long and hard if i should include this but if you've read the book of the ancestor trilogy i just wanna tell you that you won't find nona that assassin stuff here :)))

edit: SOMEBODY NEEDS TO MAKE A DIRECTORY OF ALL THE SLY BOOK REFERENCES MADE IN THIS BOOK, I'M QUESTIONING EVERYTHINGGG

title: Jade War
author: Fonda Lee
review:
brotherrrrr. wow. i cannot even can. simply amazing. i heard before i started that this second book in the series makes this series, so i was excited, but boyyy did it exceed my already inflated expectations. how did fonda lee do this? the geopolitical dynamics, the class disparities, the diaspora experiences and so much more; fonda lee makes you feel exactly how somebody would feel living in a nation set in a real fleshed out world. and to do all that with neither info dumps nor detracting from character work at all, phew, legend. i loved it. hilo is climbing the ranks of my favourite grey characters. i can't help but enjoy all the characters no matter how annoying they are sometimes. so real. that's it really, this series feels so real, so fleshed out. no other series (except maybe malazan, but in a different way) truly conveys the scale of a real earth sized world. it feels real. it feels truly real. cannot wait to binge the next book byeeeee.

i cannnnottt wait for green bone communism

title: Jade City
author: Fonda Lee
review: i see you fonda lee, i see what you're putting down and i CANNOT wait to see how everything is gonna play out. for someone who does not relate to/understand hilo much, i think hilo has potential to be one of my all time favourite characters.

title: Assassin's Quest
author: Robin Hobb
review: for the first time i understood what people said about robin hobb taking her sweet time. simple, beautiful prose, yes. extremely nuanced characters, yes. but the pace got to me for the first time. but that just means what would’ve been a 6-star trilogy dropped to a 5-star. but damn. easy 5 stars. it will break you a little, but i loved the ending. i like the magic system. love the political intrigue and the game of thrones and it's neat conclusion. as a trilogy the ending is good, much better than i expected for a trilogy that is part of a 16 book series. this series is true, raw, real, beautiful fantasy — the best. cannot wait to come back to the realm of the elderlings. soon. very soon.

title: Royal Assassin
author: Robin Hobb
review: okay, i get the robin hobb hype. shame on me for not having allowed myself to witness this earlier tbh. bro, the goosebumps. what is this writing? how are words so simple literally making me feel so physically? feelings choking my throat. sobs and tears. but also so randomly funny sometimes. and this isn't just towards the ending, this is just a few pages in onwards. oh, and i don't think i've read anything that reminded me so much of young love; ok, not just young love, but just the sheer stupid-young-angst-anger-growing-pain-frustration. in completely different setting, but the same feelings. robin hobb a genius. truly. the character work, and especially the character relationship. phew. kettricken. patience. burrich. fitz, oh fitz. i have way too many highlights to add them all to the quotes but maybe someday after i've binged the whole series i'll come back and add them, or maybe i'll look for a physical copy just so that i can annotate it to oblivion.

title: Assassin's Apprentice
author: Robin Hobb
review:
usually my reviews are my rambly notes strung together; rambly notes i write down each time something noteworthy happens. but for this book, by the second/third chapter itself i realised i can't get away with that because there were too many. so easy to read, but hits so deep! its mysterious and magical and so very human! easiest recommendation tbh, and you don't even need to read more than the first few chapters to know if you're gonna love it or not, imo

and what a closing paragraph –
"Of one other I must speak, one dragged into that conflict and intrigue only by his loyalty to me. To the end of my days, I will bear the scars he gave me. His worn teeth sank deeply into my hand several times before he managed to drag me from that pool. How he did it, I will never know. But his head still rested on my chest when they found us; his mortal bonds to this world had broken. Nosy was dead. I believe he gave his life freely, recalling that we had been good to one another when we were puppies. Men cannot grieve as dogs do. But we grieve for many years."

title: We Have Always Lived in the Castle
author: Shirley Jackson
review: silly merricat. i’m overcome by an overwhelming feeling of nostalgia and feeling like a naive silly child (but wait, merricat is 18, what, i'm so confused, so silly). what is this feeling? unsettling feeling? but it’s also ... nice? unsettling but nice? i like it. it's weird and it's weird that i like it. but i like it. is this what gothic literature is supposed to make you feel? what a gentle and mysterious kinda crazy. just my kind of weird. wow. what even was that? i wanna reread this many many times. any day i have 3-4 hours to spare and i feel the need for a nice, unsettling, mysterious, gentle escape, i'm gonna come back to merricat, constance and this house. see you soon.

title: Slow Productivity: The Lost Art of Accomplishment Without Burnout
author: Cal Newport
review:
traditional productivity (in agriculture or factory work) is defined as meaningfully valuable output per unit of capital or labor. but even productivity research doesn’t have a clear definition for what productivity means in knowledge work. three principles: do fewer things, work at a natural pace and obsess over quality.

interesting thoughts but not my cuppa tea at the moment ig. it’s no deep work. a 2-star self-help book is just want i needed to get me into a fiction reading spree though (i hope).

title: Holy Sister
author: Mark  Lawrence
review:
okay, fine. i binged it. i woke up, and devoured it. wasn't a fan of the first half of the book, again, but the avalanche of action, character moments and plot progression in the latter half always makes up for it ughhh. but this one, especially with the frequent POV switching between the past and present (nearly every chapter in the beginning) was too annoying to give it a full 5 star, as much as the ended made me want to. but, the ending, wow, love the characters, love the premise. it won't be anytime soon but i can't wait to return to mark lawrence and the focus moon. see you soon. until then, bye.

4.5/5

title: Grey Sister
author: Mark  Lawrence
review: ahh, here we go again, another 5 star. it starts alright, interesting, but just alright but the last quarter of the book?? pheewwwieee~ especially the last few ACTION packed chapters! i cannot! i have to binge the last book right now! RIGHT NOW! also, ngl it ended (the minor ending after we witnessed the ending ending) exactly how i thought it was gonna hehe. byeee, gonna start the third and final book (it's wild that these books get shorter as the series progresses!!)

title: Red Sister
author: Mark  Lawrence
review:
wowza mark lawrence, ggwp, probably the best prologue/first chapter combo i've ever been hit with, what a hook. there's no way you read the first 10 pages and not continue reading. the first sentence itself was one of the best hooks,
It is important, when killing a nun, to ensure that you bring an army of sufficient size. For Sister Thorn of the Sweet Mercy Convent, Lano Tacsis brought two hundred men.

but the feels of the first chapter is what actually got me.

but it wasn't just the hook, i loved the world concept, the characters, the plot so far. it feels reductive to compare it to the some common-ish tropes, because the excursion is so much more than the tropes. loved it. can't wait to binge the rest of the trilogy!

title: Piranesi
author: Susanna Clarke
review:
ppppppppppppiiiiiiirrrrrrrraaaaaaannnnnneeeeessssssssiiiiiii (thanks, j)

ok, i've started this book a total of three times and i felt soo lost in the first few pages that i put it down the first two times. but yesterday, i was on a flight for roughly the exact time i expected to take reading this book so i thought i'd just go for it, and woaahhwiieee am i glad i did. i was sooo lost, once again, but this time IN THE BEST WAY POSSIBLE. i loved it. the mystery. the suspense. chillssss, literal chillssss. 10/10, would recommend getting lost in this book. i loved it. also instantly putting it in my reread pile for when i, inevitably, want to experience this world again (it reminded me of my childhood, in a similar way to how starless sea made me feel; i don't know how else to verbalise this). ok byeeeeeeeeaaaannnneeessssiii.

title: The Voyage of the Dawn Treader
author: C.S. Lewis

title: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe
author: C.S. Lewis

title: Thornhedge
author: T. Kingfisher
review:
what a beautiful concept, what beautiful writing, cutting, raw and real. t kingfisher what a genius.
“I am not beautiful ... No. But you are interesting. And sad.”
“He likes stories. And I know so many. And he says his mother is kind.”

title: The Fury of the Gods
author: John Gwynne
review:
CATHARSIS! that's what this book was. yes, it hurt me. yes, i cried. yes, my standards for high fantasy is forever altered. BUT, IT WAS CATHARTIC UGGHHHH. VINDICATIONNN! i loved the writing, so raw, so visceral, it felt like i heard, felt, experienced the action. i'mma speak about a few very very spoiler-y plot moments that were written exceptionally (and ruined me).


the last few sentences we get in elvar's pov –
“There was an impact on her neck, like a punch.
“No,” she heard Biórr’s voice shout.
She opened her mouth to speak, but could not, choking on something in her throat. She coughed, hot blood gushing out in an explosion from her mouth. Then the hand holding her head was gone, other hands pushing her, and she was stumbling towards the edge of the stairwell, legs weak, falling. Distantly, as if from a far way off, she heard Grend scream.”

like i said, how is this man making me feel good about how beautiful the writing is when i am hating what's happening within the writing ughhh.

also, hold up a min, was there effectively no foreshadowing about grend being tainted (i mean, yes, we know he was special, but we also know elvar was special, but elvar can't narratively be tainted, so i never thought grend could be tainted) OR AM I JUST STUPID?? (chapter 33)

hehehe, also also, until i was wondering if that iskidan invasion storyline just going to be left open for a future sequel or something and then we see all the fleet coming in AND THEN WE SEE SNAKA JUST WHIP THEM OUT i was dying laughing.

(chapter 54) see, narratively, i knew it was INEVITABLE, elvar freeing the thralls, but i cried just a teeny bit anyways.

man, the back to back to back chapter where we get TRUE VINDICATION, it was unreal, I LOVED IT, I LIVED FOR IT. BRAK GOT WHAT HE DESERVED — cathartic doesn’t even begin to cover it. COULDN'T WAIT for gudvarr’s end. AND EXCEEDS EXPECTATIONS. Vindication after vindication after vindication. AND THE CHERRY ON TOP! THORKEL AVENGED! ORKA is now officially my FAVOURITE character in all of fiction! mother orka!

OK. i'm not gonna name then all and cry all over again BUT THE WAR/CLASH WE LOST A LOT OF THEM AND JUST WHEN WE THINK THAT'S IT THE REST OF US MADE IT WE LOSE GLORNIR NOOOOOOOOOO!

but but but, the last few chapters, the ending. i'm floored. perfection. when everyone returned to the steading, to thorkel, to bury glornir — i was in my office sobbing. what a beautiful ending. a home, a place to call their own, kin, warm fire, young arms, peace, a home. everything about the closing chapters was perfection.

P.S. shoutout to “I choose you” by varg and rokia — literal chillssss


this trilogy is an experience. heartbreak. vindication. it gave me everything i wanted and more. 10/10. top tier. easy recommendation. also thanks for the recommendation. ilysm. ok bye!

title: The Hunger of the Gods
author: John Gwynne
review: AAAAAAARRRGHHHH OH MY MY GOODNESS what did i just read?!?!? i cannot, i simply cannot can. all the POVs and different plot lines i spoke about in the first book’s review has coalesced into an epic saga here. i do not think i’m capable of reviewing the first few chapters because once it had me THE WAY I DEVOURED THIS BOOK ughhh. i love the character work. i love how visceral(?) and sensual(?) his writing is when we’re in our character’s heads. i love the combat. THE LAST FEW CHAPTERS!!! GAGGED!!! the payoff’s were crrrrrazy, i mean it did cross my mind if it’s just hype and wish fulfilment but nopes, i loved it. this is what i want, just what i need, gimme more. the ending is CRIMINAL. a moment of silence for all of yall who had to wait for the third book to release after that ending. k bye, gonna go start fury of the gods right away.

title: The Shadow of the Gods
author: John Gwynne
review: i am reviewing this later than my usual as-soon-as-i-finish-the-last-page rant because i wanted to make sure the 5-star i wanted to give it was absolutely free of external influence (who am i kidding) BUT truly, the way i was SO ANNOYED at the start of this book because of how the POVs switched EVERY SINGLE chapter, and each POV is covering three distinct plot-lines and settings. so. 3 POVs. 3 settings. 3 sets of characters. EACH CHAPTER is reasonably sized, not too long, not too short, but leaning shorter than most. I usually HATE this because I end up liking one POV and have to fight the urge to skim the others to get back to my preferred POV, BUT BUT BUT this book oh my goodness, by around the half way point I DIDN'T KNOW WHICH POV I WANTED TO READ, ALL OF THEM, ALL THE TIME, AT THAT POINT I'M JUST BINGING THE BOOK i loved it, i loved, thanks for the rec, wow, can't wait to binge the rest of the trilogy, i love it. rant fin.

title: Everything Is Tuberculosis: The History and Persistence of Our Deadliest Infection
author: John  Green
review:
”TB in the twenty first century is not really caused by a bacteria that we know how to kill. TB in the twenty first century is really caused by [human injustice].”


a truth as true as this quote is that i can’t do justice to this book or to tuberculosis patients with these few words, but you need to understand: you need to read this. if you don’t listen to any of my other asks, just read this. it’s a sin i don’t tend to partake in — short book summaries — but for this one i need to, because for the love of all that’s good you need to know at least pointers, because i didn’t:

tuberculosis — a deadly killer and still our (humanity’s) deadliest diseases — is curable. diagnosis and cure research boomed when rich, white people were sick; but it’s still often not considered “cost-effective” to cure the poor. there’s hesitation to treat drug-resistant (DR TB) variants out of fear of breeding more resistance — we’re effectively at four stages of drug resistance — but failing to cure DR TB only lets it spread. that contradiction is exactly why we need sustained public funding for research to keep up with TB’s strategic drug evasion.

i cried more reading this book than any fiction i’ve read in forever. also, marco polo chapter ending goes hardddd. thank you john green for using your megaphone. thank you henry.

“TB doesn’t just flow through the meandering river of injustice; TB broadens and deepens that river.”

title: Vera Wong's Unsolicited Advice for Murderers
author: Jesse Q. Sutanto
review: what a cute lil silly lil goofy lil found-family-wholesome lil book. took a while to warm up to it. slow start, but the perfect breather i needed from my malazan read. easy binge. easy good feels. to quote the book itself, “vera is quite possibly the most vexing person he has ever met. but somehow, he can’t not see her as an honorary mother figure in his life.”

title: House of Chains
author: Steven Erikson
review:
the last set of chapters tried its best to redeem this book, but after memories of ice, this fell flat for me. or maybe i just really missed the bridgeburners, and karsa's arc didn't land with me. i've heard people say they're fans of the trull sengar and onrack dynamic, but i didn't see that either. but then, as has been with this series so far, i'll probably only vibe with them after a few books—like how i was meh about the bridgeburners in gardens of the moon but now i can't get enough of them. memories of ice made me wanna go right into the next book, but now i think i'm gonna go back to taking a break before the next book in the series.

3.5/5

title: Sunrise on the Reaping
author: Suzanne Collins
review:
this book was actually much better than ballad of songbirds and snakes. right from the start. i'm not biased. trust me. its not because i like the characters (old and new) better either. it wasn’t just nostalgia bait, the character and their arcs were good. this actually delivered a lot of what i hoped for in a prequel for the hunger games (which balled unfortunately disappointed). the hunger games formula for similar enough but still fresh, and the characters and writing is new an fresh. loved the references, i read the original series last ages ago so i’m sure i missed a few but still was a fun time. i loved how much of mags we got. maysilee fav of course. man, i wish we had more of her. a sister you fight with and a sister you fight for. oof. ngl in my ballad review i said i blame the expectation but no, this is what i wanted from a hunger games prequel ya know what i mean. so idk if i wanna read more prequels. and the ending was so good. the epilogue itself deserves an extra half star though hehe.

4.5/5

p.s. very dungeon-crawler-carl-esque but carl is hunger-games-esque so idek but yknow

title: The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes
author: Suzanne Collins
review: boohoo privileged/royalty boy grows up in poverty and gets his heart broken so now everyone except him must suffer. i love the hunger games. i wanted snow to be so much more. sad. my expectations were probably to blame tbh.

title: Memories of Ice
author: Steven Erikson
review:
man, how do you review a malazan book? even when it’s just the ranty nonsense like i usually do?

usually when i read back through my reviews, i remember most, if not all, the major plot and character beats. but i went through my deadhouse gates reviews, and i remember the chain of dogs feeling, but not the details, and i hate it. i hate it because the review i’m tempted to write for this, memories of ice, will probably meet the same fate ... i just know it. yet i want to do it anyways and hope, against odds, the deets stays with me. i blame the sheer amount of things that happen on each page tbh. my goodness. but here goes. ugh.

mostly shout-outs to characters and moments. and spoilers ahead.



quick ben.
toc the younger.
and the weird funny friendships. loved rake x whiskeyjack. loved toc x tool. loved gruntle x paran. loved gruntle x itkovian. loved gruntle x toc. okay maybe i just like gruntle. and ttkovian. and paran. and whiskeyjack. and toc. and the bridgeburners. and EVERYBODY OMGGGG.

when Quick Ben did the
Oh, one more thing, Hood. You and your fellow gods have been calling out the rules uncontested for far too long. Step back, now, and see how us mortals fare ... I think you're in for a surprise or two.
CHILLSSSS.

what ever is Kruppe broooo.

i don’t wanna jinx myself but … is … malazan … starting to make sense???? (i hope i don’t eat my words when i come back after months to the next book; but tbf this time i think i’ll be back much sooner than my earlier breaks.)

OKAY. my god Itkovian. wow. what a true Shield Anvil. now i understand what a Shield Anvil should be.

NOOO WHISKEYJACK NOOOOOOOO
KORLAT NOOO.
MAN. ITKOVIAN. and to think i was annoyed to read his POV at the beginning (among so many other POVs). and same with Gruntle.



NOW I UNDERSTAND WHY PEOPLE REREAD. ONLY THEN WILL YOU GENUINELY KNOW WHICH PEOPLE TO EXPERIENCE MORE DEEPLY UGHHHH. why erikson whyyy.

OMFG Toc and Tool.
Tool ending.
what an ending.

first true proper 5/5 malazan books so far

title: The Midnight Library
author: Matt Haig
review: i highly recommend it, and i highly recommend going in without knowing what the book is about at all. such beauty, such pain, such feels — what a meaningful beautiful read. bye.

title: Tress of the Emerald Sea
author: Brandon Sanderson
review:
man who doesn’t love a really good, fun, fresh hero’s journey? but also who’d have thunk sanderson/hoid would write such a romantic motivation and also such delicious weird comedy (and by delicious i mean the cringiest!! and i love it) he truly embraces the whimsy
“she wasn’t even certain she was tress anymore, or if she’d become someone else. you could say, in other words, that her state at the moment was distress.”

and not just cringe humour but also so many quoteable insights wew
“but if you ask these heroes why they risked their lives, don’t do it on a stand in front of a crowd while you give them their medal. because the truth is, they likely didn’t do it for their country. or even for their ideals. consistently, across cultures, eras, and ideologies, war heroes report the same simple motivation. they did it for their friends.”
“it is a great irony that society tends to look down on those who sell their bodies, but not on those who lease out their minds.”

truly feels like a fairy tale written for grown ups (or whatever i am)

truly loved that it makes hoid sooo real, as in hoid as a storyteller so truly and purely unhinged hoid energy idk bro i know a lotta of yall figured huck was charlie but i didn’t have a clue lol rip

if you know hoid 5/5, else 4/5 iykyk

title: The Secret History
author: Donna Tartt
review:
this story really is gonna live in my head for a long time, arguably forever
i don’t think i’ll ever know how to describe how this book makes me feel
writing so immersive
i know it’s not saying much because i just finished it, but i think i’m gonna keep thinking about this story again and again for who knows how long

4.5/5 — only because part 2 dragged a little but it was also very needed so idk but ya just feels like a 4.5

title: Storm Front
author: Jim  Butcher
review:
hehe idk sorry if it's super reductive, but i mean it as a compliment when i say i felt the same way i used to feel when i read famous five or secret seven as a kid, but y'know, with magic, real monsters, and more horrifyingly (i'm kidding) adults. will eventually come back to the series, not in the mood at the moment.

3.5/5

title: The Anthropocene Reviewed: Essays on a Human-Centered Planet
author: John  Green
review:
what a truly, deeply human experience, reading about somebody’s passions and opinions. i related more to the way he thinks than i learned things per se, but truly an experience. i think everyone should read this. and i think everyone should write reviews about things they’ve gone down rabbit holes for or are deeply passionate about (including passionate hate).
"everything I say and hear has to drip through the sieve of my anxiety"

title: Show Your Work!: 10 Ways to Share Your Creativity and Get Discovered
author: Austin Kleon

title: This Inevitable Ruin
author: Matt Dinniman
review:
oh my god, after the last book giving peck found family vibes i should’ve known this was gonna be even better, but i wasn’t prepared. when the past crawlers showed up and the hup hup hup war chant started for carl i was crying, very katniss mockingjay moment. and phew, the 9th floor blood sultanate has been hanging over our heads for the whole series since book one and i loved the resolution! on that note (and only that note) i think now is a good time to binge the series because a lot of plotlines resolve here. i cannot even imagine how the others who had to wait for this book lived. there are tons more answers i need and i can’t wait for the future but we at least got a few resolutions.

4.5/5. only because for the first time in this series the start dragged a little, had me distracted. inevitable new characters and exposition ig but still. and wasn’t a fan of the past crawler flashback chapters in between the main chapters just like i wasn't a fan of the interludes in stormlight archive. that’s to say i liked the value it adds but i do not like the interruption to the main storyline. edit: okay nvm i take it back, now that it’s not flashbacks, these chapters bang.

title: The Eye of the Bedlam Bride
author: Matt Dinniman
review:
this book singlehandedly (lol who am i kidding) ruined my sleep schedule because found family stories always have me in a chokehold. gah.

when all our crawlers SHOWED UP TOGETHER for carl and princess at the end?? i'm crying. truly truly tears and smiles.

it reeaally just keeps getting better. honestly this deserves 5 stars more than the earlier books even though i rated few of them 5 stars. can't wait to read the next one. my goodness, after the next one i'll have to wait FOREVER (matt dinniman's really good with his release schedule looks like hopefully he keeps it up) for the next one noooooooooooooooo.

title: And Every Morning the Way Home Gets Longer and Longer
author: Fredrik Backman
review: every short book i’ve read this year has had me in tears gah my goodness

title: The Butcher's Masquerade
author: Matt Dinniman
review: miiight just be my favourite dungeon crawler carl book so far. tons of heavy hitters in this one. fortify they heart, for there will be feels. possibly.

title: The Gate of the Feral Gods
author: Matt Dinniman
review: i love it! but the main story line is getting too interesting and the fun crawl is getting in the way ugh sooo many more floors to go! it's getting bigger, and better, and sooo much more ... interesting

title: The Burning God
author: R.F. Kuang
review:
okay so i just finished the burning god yesterday and i am not okay. i feel like rfk came to my house and beat me up, gave me a hug and whispered “it’s war babe” before disappearing into the fog.


live notes: i just started the last pre epilogue chapter. everything that felt powerful, righteous, even good just crumbled into absolute absolute ruin. ugh. this can't be real.

and that ENDING???? no no noooOOO. i’m literally in denial. i finished it and just sat there like “this cannot be the end. run it back. i demand a do-over.” but nope. it’s done. and now i’m about to fall down a rabbit hole of booktube deep dives and reddit conspiracy threads trying to make peace with the chaos. (and also tumble down wikipedia rabbit hole finding irl parallels this trilogy pulled from.)

like it was so stupid. and wonderful. and terrible. and perfect. and i ended fast. WAY too fast. i wanted to see the after, the fallout, the healing (or lack of it), the reckoning. but war doesn’t always give you that, huh.


anyway. 10/10 series. would recommend. would not recover. thanks i hate it i love it.

title: The Dragon Republic
author: R.F. Kuang
review:
oh my goodness. oh my goodness. ugh. the first book was good, but this one? no words. i'm actually pissed (yes, i do understand that most of the five-starred reviews i give tend to be books that piss me off) and this one? i was not emotionally prepared. it’s a problem. a real problem. oh my goodness.

the north isn't better than the south. the colonisers and the sects that aligned with the colonisers are absolute trash. i'm so annoyed. ughhhh. i hope they get what's due to them in the next book. guess i have to binge it too now.

title: The Six Deaths of the Saint
author: Alix E. Harrow

title: Every Heart a Doorway
author: Seanan McGuire
review:
wow...just wow. initially i thought wow this book is everything (literally everything) i'd have loved as a teen, and then surprise surprise it's everything i love still. easy read. loved all the characters. i don't think i want to read any more books in the series. this one is enough. it's enough. my heart is full.

(slightly spoilor-y) edit: we need more romantic asexual main characters.

title: Yellowface
author: R.F. Kuang
review: this has got to be the most frustrating book i have ever read, i hated every minute of reading it, truly, deeply, utterly hated it. but i loved how much rfk made me hate it. i would picture her cackling while writing it. i hated june with a passion. and just when i thought it could not get worse, chapter 18 happens, where she is teaching high schoolers and she straight up bullies and gaslights her students. that is it. worst human possible. you win. my most hated character. and my most hated character arc. i binged it in a single sitting. 5 stars. ugh.

review: funny, ridiculous, chaotic, bat-sh*t crazy, absurd, occasionally icky, always unhinged, quick-paced, but also sooo much promise for serious political intrigue in the future books, but again it's sooo stupid, i love it! and i should've listened to the audiobook from the beginning, the narration by jeff hays and the audio production is so much fun! all hail princess donut, byeeee

title: The Communist Manifesto
author: Karl Marx
review: How do you even review this one? Just a few heads-ups if you're reading this edition (or most commonly available editions): Part 1, which is an introduction by Gareth Stedman Jones, and the footnotes make up nearly 75% of the book. Sure, I learned a lot from the context they provided, but I wasn’t mentally prepared for what, in my opinion, was (dry) academic writing—not exactly what I enjoy in my spare time. If I were to rate that 75% of the book, I’d give it only 2 stars. But the reason I picked up this book was Part 2—the manifesto itself—which was a solid read, 4 stars, especially the first two chapters. I highly recommend reading the manifesto. It won’t take more than an hour or two, and honestly, in my opinion, you don’t need much more context unless you’re studying it academically or want to dive deeper into philosophy or philosophical history.

author: Evan Winter

title: Regarding the Pain of Others
author: Susan Sontag
review:
Must read. Everything Sontag talks about, photographing and televising war, is manifold with the current social media and internet news-ification. Sontag explores how war photography shapes the understanding of those who haven't experienced war. The copy I read already had tons of underlines and highlights, and ngl, it has sooo many quote-worthy sentences, but:

"'We'—this 'we' is everyone who has never experienced anything like what they went through—don't understand. We don't get it. We truly can't imagine what it was like. We can't imagine how dreadful, how terrifying war is; and how normal it becomes. Can't understand, can't imagine. That's what every soldier, and every journalist and aid worker and independent observer who has put in time under fire, and had the luck to elude the death that struck down others nearby, stubbornly feels. And they are right."

title: Last Stand of the Stone Fist
author: Michael R.  Miller
review:
pls i want more pls it was too short pls why novella gots to be so short pls

pain

title: Defiant
author: Michael R.  Miller
review: Wow. Just wow. I just finished reading this, and it was absolutely incredible, just wow. MRM truly outdoes himself here – this feels like the pinnacle of Songs of Chaos so far. I get it now, I get why it's called Songs of Chaos. This book and the plot gets so chaotic in the best way possible, I loved it. If the remaining two books in the series are anything like this one, omg, I can't wait. From the beginning soo much happens. A lesser author might have bloating this into multiple books, but this book feels like an epic trilogy condensed into a single, tightly woven masterpiece wow. It’s an intense and satisfying experience. Don't get me wrong, this book flirts with almost grimdark levels of failure for our characters, and not everything is as wholesome as the magic system is. But the balance is perfect, and the redemption arc for you-know-who has my heart. If more progression fantasy were this wholesome, phew that's all I'd be reading. I can't wait for Reckoning – can't wait, can't wait, can't wait!

title: Unbound
author: Michael R.  Miller
review:
I loved it, but, the POV switching in the first few chapters was painful—it was really hard to build momentum with each plotline. If the objective was to make me keep reading just to return to the original POV, and by the end of the new POV make me want more of that one, so I end reading through the original POV to get back to the new one (idek if that makes sense), then congratulations—you succeeded. I was hooked. But I didn't enjoy feeling manipulated, thank you very much. Also also what stood out to me most was how, unlike many fantasy main characters, Holt genuinely struggles with adventuring basics like hunting for food, swordsmanship, etc. He’s a cook, and his struggles endeared him to me in a way that many YA fantasy protagonists don’t. Very relatable writing ngl! I still love the hard magic system, and overall good stuff, already starting the next book.

[3.5/5]

title: The Fire Next Time
author: James Baldwin
review: please read this

title: Ascendant
author: Michael R.  Miller
review:
Let’s go, besties—we are soo back! I absolutely loved this. It’s been way too long since I couldn’t put a book down, y’know? We're so back, binge reading and all. The hard magic system was awesome—wholesome isn’t a word I ever thought I’d use to describe a magic system, but wow, wholesome. If this is just a sample of what’s to come in Songs of Chaos, sign me up. I’m officially a fan. A boy, his recipe book, and a blind dragon take on a classist, elitist, blight-ridden world? Say less. Familiar tropes done so well. Also, I loved how the story portrays the emotions behind growing, living with a disability. If you’ve ever wanted to feel the way you did reading Eragon for the first time or watching How to Train Your Dragon, this is an easy recommendation. It’s not a retelling by any means, but it captures that feels. YA fantasy at its best.

Edit: I have since learned Michael R. Miller spoke about his cystic fibrosis and how he drew from it to describe Ash’s disability—it really shows. The breathing techniques riders use to cleanse their dragons’ cores (a part of the magic system I called wholesome) are directly inspired by his own physiotherapy treatments for cystic fibrosis.

[4.5/5]

title: The God of Small Things
author: Arundhati Roy
review:
Unease. Constant, unyielding unease. That’s what I felt throughout this book. What I don't understand is why I still loved it? From the beginning, even though I already knew what was going to happen, I had a constant morbid premonition that something terrible was always just around the corner. It was so uncomfortable. I kept telling myself how uneasy I felt as I was reading it. Why did it feel more grim dark than anything grim dark I’ve read? And randomly, in the midst of all that uneasiness, I was constantly awed by how relatable the writing of child minds and child thoughts was? I avoided this novel for years, probably fearing this unease—though I don’t think I knew exactly what it was about. Sure, it made me uneasy, constantly. But maybe I needed that unease? We need that unease? Because people still live with this unease? It’s not fair to run away from it.

Also—congrats, Baby Kochamma might now be my most hated character of all time?

BONUS:

Quote from an Arundhati Roy interview about the book:

“It made many middle-class people in India, the heart of the book-reading public, uncomfortable, angry, sometimes furious with me. Because the way I see the world does not allow people to let themselves off the hook; it leaves little space for pleading innocence. And it’s uncomfortable to face the fact that all of us are complicit in what’s going on—victims as well as perpetrators … Estha and Rahel, though they were only seven years old, knew, and were honest enough to admit that they were in some way complicit in the violence that was unleashed on Velutha. Adults find that hard to do.”
—From an interview with Arundhati Roy


My favorite quote from the book:

“Thirty-one.
Not old.
Not young.
But a viable die-able age.”
—From The God of Small Things


Estha. Rahel. Velutha. Ammu ... My Ammukutty from Ayemenem.

title: The Covenant of Water
author: Abraham   Verghese
review:
I finished this book a week ago, and I still can’t stop thinking about it. Honestly, I’ve been reeling ever since. I’ve asked all my close friends to read it because I just need someone to talk to about it. Reading the last few chapters, I literally threw my kindle down and paced around the room—multiple times!

The book was almost a love letter to both Kerala and the art of medicine. The story gives such a rich insight into early 1900s domestic life in Kerala, and the attention to historical detail is incredible. The plot though goes rogue. Man, it goes rogueee! But it’s all woven into historically accurate settings (as far as I can tell from the references), which makes it feel that much more immersive.

As someone from Travancore, where most of the story is set, I'm maybe, possibly, a teeny weeny bit biased. But reading about a place that feels like home, through such vivid, visceral storytelling, made this so much better for me. Easy 5 stars. And also, as an engineer, I never thought I’d feel even the tiniest pang of regret about not studying or practicing medicine—until now. Abraham Verghese’s writing really brings the art of medicine to life oof I cannot even.

Honestly, The Covenant of Water is a phenomenal start to my reading year. Highly recommend. Must read. Happy reading!

author: Chetan Bhagat

title: Jerusalem: The Biography
author: Simon Sebag Montefiore
review: Hard to pick up if you put it down, but once you pick it up it's super hard to put back down. 3-star rating only because I’m no expert and cannot vouch for its accuracy or fair representation. Nevertheless, in my opinion, it read as unbiased. I enjoyed reading it, and I learned lotta new facts that sent me down one too many google/wikipedia rabbit holes. Most of it read like a novel than a textbook—a good read and a great place to start learning.

title: Crying in H Mart
author: Michelle Zauner
review: This is the first memoir I’ve ever read, and that makes it both so much better and so much worse. It hurts so bad, but feels so good. I love to read real, raw representations of grief and emotional descriptions of mouthwatering food. Unlike the usual fiction I tend to read, where I search for good portrayals of grief, this was raw by default—and I didn’t know how to feel or how to process it while writing this. From the first chapter, I’ve been reading this book with blurry vision. From the first chapter, I’ve been drooling over and pining for good food while holding back tears. Big ouch. I sound cavalier, but I just finished it, and now I might be bawling. Goodnight.

title: Deadhouse Gates
author: Steven Erikson
review:
Erikson, why do you do this to me? The chain of dogs broke me– the tragedy of war, the weight of collective suffering, the mere thread of hope, ugh, I cannot, my heart, ugh. Mappo and Icarium, and my poor dear historian soldier Duiker.

The book was darker than my usual, but it’s not exactly grimdark. It’s brutal and heavy, dark without even trying hard to be dark. It's a kinda effortless, natural brutality, just showing things as they are rather than spelling them out, it makes it all hit so much harder. And bruh, the dark humor, (I'm guessing is) the coping mechanism soldiers would use mid tragedy idek? I didn’t want to laugh, but Erikson doesn’t care, ugh! It read like a classical tragedy where everyone loses, and everything hurts.

This book cost me my whole weekend. It was an experience! 5+ stars. I can't wait to read the remaining eight books in the series but it's so long and so heavy, and I don't know if I can take it back to back but also the idea of taking a break, coming back later, and forgetting too much– it’s terrifying/frustrating/annoying. Thank God for the chapter wise recaps.

title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
author: Yuval Noah Harari
review:
Not gonna lie, this book wasn’t what I expected. I really enjoyed The Cognitive Revolution (Part One)—4.5/5. But The Agricultural Revolution and The Unification of Humankind sections? Eh, mid. They felt a bit too philosophical, speculative and preachy, especially compared to the vibe of the first part.

That said, the Scientific Revolution section had some gems! The Discovery of Ignorance, The Marriage of Science and Empire, and The Capitalist Creed chapters had pretty cool takes.

Note to future me: If you ever re-read this or recommend it to someone, stick to Part One: The Cognitive Revolution and those chapters from Part Four: Scientific Revolution.

title: ഖസാക്കിന്റെ ഇതിഹാസം | Khasakkinte Ithihasam | The Legends of Khasak
author: ഒ.വി.വിജയൻ | O.V.Vijayan
review:
It's a pity that Khasakkinte Ithihasam was introduced to me as "what One Hundred Years of Solitude's Macondo was to Aracataca is what Khasakkinte Ithihasam's Khasak was to Thasarak." I mean, I get it. My head hurt just as much reading this as it did when I was reading One Hundred Years of Solitude, and I by no means understood nearly enough of book, but I still think I got a lot more of the implied subtext because, unlike Macondo, Khasak is nammude swantham (well, kinda).

Throughout the time I was reading (or after the prose got too hard and I switched to the audiobook), I was going around randomly saying Allappicha Mollakka, much like I would randomly sprout out José Arcadio Buendía. And I get it, okay—I know I am referencing One Hundred Years of Solitude a lot for someone who opened this review the way I did.

I don't think I've ever read anything else that portrayed guilt better. Though not my favorite, I liked the portrayal of betrayal (I think? Because it do be abstract, nah mean).

And hi, I know you're reading this and you don't read malayalam—the english translation (though there are some differences) was written by the original author O. V. Vijayan, and if you want a peek into a magical-realism-esque take on Kerala, add it to your to-read list. (And if you think you do read malayalam and are stubborn like I was, when the palakkadan-tamil-malayalam prose inevitably gets impossible, the free audiobooks on youtube are awesome—the book is head-hurting enough without having to decode the words) thank you alisha

...is this...the longest review I've written? allappicha mollakka byeeee

title: The Perks of Being a Wallflower
author: Stephen Chbosky

title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
author: Gabrielle Zevin
review: Man, these two (and Marx) – friends, often in love but never lovers, are truly genius game developers. I haven't done the research, but if most of the game ideas explored and developed in this story aren't real games or variations of games, then Gabrielle Zevin has more mind-blowing game ideas than I ever could. And that too as a subplot within a book? Sheesh. Gamers, programmers, lovers, friends (and angsty readers)—maybe give this one a shot. Once I got a few pages in, it had to be binged. Thanks for the recommendation.

title: A Natural History of Dragons
author: Marie Brennan
review: Ngl I need to rethink my rating structure because this is better than most/all of my 4-stars but not necessarily guaranteed reread i.e. 5-star. Solid 4.5 star. Lady Trent feels so real that I almost feel like I could write to her or meet her at a Q&A or attend her lecture or smt. It's fantasy but through a scientific lens, but not at all like a textbook. It is set in a Victorian-inspired world very similar to our world. Even if you're not into fantasy but enjoy Jane Austen and maybe wanna check out fantasy, you should check this out. It's very cozy, and I can't wait to come back to the series after a while to vibe with Lady Trent again.

title: Deep Work: Rules for Focused Success in a Distracted World
author: Cal Newport

title: The Last Enemy: Dark Marks
author: C.H. Darling
review: The final chapter released today, just as I was finishing my read-through. Wow. It's political. Many of the political topics are relevant to the 1970s and the world, but there's something universal about students banding together to fight (and die in a war) against fascism. It resonates across the years, nations, and contexts, and that hits hard. It's awesome. I mean, obviously, there are the advertised slow burns and angst, but this is my new headcanon for all the other reasons also. Because it makes the fight feel real and relatable in an almost scary way. It's book two out of four, I believe, and I'll have to get back to this series in about 5/6 years and catch up because I noticed how the chapters tend to end on cliffhangers, and my heart can't take that week-to-week/month-to-month. I love it. Thank you, CH Darling. Congrats on finishing book two, and thanks again.

title: The Last Enemy: The Howling Nights
author: C.H. Darling
review: I don't usually log fanfiction on goodreads, but C.H. Darling might just be redefining my marauder-era headcanons. This book, set in the marauders' sixth year really does them justice as complex, flawed, and utterly human characters, and I love me some complex character work. It made me feel. Can't wait for the rest of the books. Even if you're not usually into fanfic, easy entry recommendation, this one. It's been ages since I binged a book in a day, and I've missed it. It's back baby. Let's goooo!

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
author: Gabriel García Márquez
review: This fever dream of a book made me uncomfortably aware of solitude while also connecting my existence to humanity, idk bro, it’s weird. Reluctant 4-star because I wanted for it to be over, but also for it to never end. It was uncomfortable to read, not enjoyable, but I still want more? (repeat) idk bro, it’s weird.

title: The Storied Life of A.J. Fikry
author: Gabrielle Zevin
review: “But here is what I do know. ‘A Trip to the Beach’ by Maya Tamerlane Fikry was written by a writer“ was written by a writer writing about love. Stories about bookstores, stories about found family, stories about characters, stories that you can’t forget, stories you read in a single sitting, stories you want everybody you love to read, it’s not a lot of stories. I hope I reread this, again and again. I don’t think it’s the prose nor the plot, but something about this story hit me right me the feels ok bye I go cry. @future.me — read private notes and highlights on reread.

title: Gardens of the Moon
author: Steven Erikson
review: I don’t understand it, but I love it (?)

title: A Memory of Light
author: Robert Jordan
review:
Man, that 13-book build-up? Worth every page! The reunion Mat x Rand x Perrin banter? Mat's comedy relief? Mat's 3 boot analogy for nobility; is Talmanes just a sneakerhead or is the analogy actually deep? Perrin's post trial power hammer forging scene? And Androl and Pevara - total wild card; I questioned why we're spending the last book meeting the 9743252846568th character, but wow, worth it? And bruh, I'd pay good money for a anime clip of Perrin's anime-style Tel'aran'rhiod throwdowns with Slayer—oof. It's just a book full of bruh moments!

I love long winded fantasy books/shows/manga/movies/anime, but The Wheel of Time is truly one of the best character work, period. Man, they hit different. So many characters, so many favourites.
And for the longest time I thought it'd be a basic chosen one trope or that Rand would be a Mary Sue; but no, not even close. Rand's arc is hands down one of my all time favourite protagonist character arcs; highest highs and the lowest of lows. And the Last Battle was a masterpiece. The huge cast each had a specific role to play, each plot-line and side tangents had a significant pay-off in the battle. Pure gold. Literally hundreds of character arcs, each doing their thing in the Last Battle, oof. Loved the characters, or loved how much I hated them, or loved how I loved them while being annoyed with them—it's like dealing with real people, you can't help but be hooked, ugh. I get it now. Took me 14 books, but I get it, I get why the Wheel of Time is considered one of the greats in fantasy, wow! “Stories have power. Gleemen’s tales, and bards’ epics, and rumors in the street alike. They stir passions, and change the way men see the world.” - Winter’s Heart

P.S. never have I ever felt like rereading a series right away while also not wanting to reread because of the sheer time commitment, ugh.

P.P.S. I know I said "worth every page!", but I'll give my final verdict after my eventual reread on if the pages where Jordan "sometimes spends a chapter talking about a curtain or a leaf..." were actually worth it.

title: Towers of Midnight
author: Robert Jordan
review: One last build-up book. Much better than the slump books, but still largely a build-up book. But, wow, the next book is going to be awesome; I can already feel it. I've felt sad about having to stay away from the fandom during my read to avoid spoilers. I would have loved to be part of the fandom as it released, but I can't imagine waiting years for the next book. Birgitte x Mat friendship, needed more. Once again, the battle in Tar Valon  was the most fun; Tel'aran'rhiod King, my boy Perrin. The suspense at the ending was wild af. Can't wait for A Memory of Light.

title: The Gathering Storm
author: Robert Jordan
review: The Gathering Storm is probably my favorite Wheel of Time book so far. Unexpectedly, I loved the White Tower arc; not just the battle, but the schemes, the tension, Egwene's painful rise to influence and power. Tam's appearance made me realise how much I usually enjoy reading father-son plotlines. We need more of Tam and Rand. I know it's unlikely we'll ever get more of Tuon x Mat exclusive chapters, but I still wish there was more of both of them. What a plot twist Verin Sedai; she was so sus from the very beginning, and the sheer size of this series made me forget my initial sus vibes. I had started taking Verin as is, but wow, truly unexpected.

title: Winter's Heart
author: Robert Jordan
review: Yes, yes, yes, at last. Books 7 and 8 were such a drag. I put down Path of Daggers more than a year back, and sheer stubbornness made me pick it back up and finish it in the last week of December; it's ending hinted at resolution, but the last 2 books had worn me down, and I didn't have many hopes. But damn, did Winter's Heart deliver. Lots of Mat, no bickering, lots of Rand, lots of action, a lot more communication, a lot more of the core cast working together without friction, and many plot points that had been painstakingly built up over the past many books were resolved. So very satisfying. Literally binged it. This is getting 4.5 stars from me purely because of the satisfaction of how much happens in this (comparatively short) book. Effectively a million words long slow burn is what it was, ugh. Gonna start Crossroads of Twilight right away; hoping the character misunderstandings, and the snail-paced world building gives way. Fingers crossed.

title: Melodrama in the Multiverse
author: GOKUL NAIR
review: I went into this expecting to enjoy Acolyte, Room 42, and everybody's favourite sentient macaroni, Bob, and I did, but brother, Minutemen and the Frampton Hypothesis caught me off guard—wow. It's not every day you can say, 'wow, my friend wrote this,' and personally tell him that you need to get back to writing.  We need more of where that came from. And also, not gonna lie, I might actually nick a few lines from Acolyte for my academic writing at some point, if things pan out, hehe. More, please. Also, I had two questions I can't ask here; I'll ask you when I see you next weekend for Christmas in London, hehe, see ya.

title: The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck: A Counterintuitive Approach to Living a Good Life
author: Mark Manson

title: Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones
author: James Clear
review: Rarely have I read a book for the first time and sensed a familiarity as if I've encountered its concepts elsewhere. The ideas explored in this book have been dissected, incorporated, and sometimes borrowed by a wide range of self improvement youtube channels and podcasts. However, Atomic Habits stands out as a comprehensive guide on the subject, covering everything from the intricacies of habits to their transformative power in shaping oneself. I highly recommend it, especially if you haven't been through a self-improvement youtube binge phase.

title: Bright Lights, Big City
author: Jay McInerney
review: At first, I thought, "oops, that's a lot of words I have to keep looking up." This is what happens when you try to read what an English Literature major suggests. It also makes me question every suggestion I've made to James

title: Ikigai: The Japanese Secret to a Long and Happy Life
author: Héctor  García
review: Breaking my non-fiction dry spell today to and officially joining the Audible gang because, let's face it, desperate times call for audiobooks to conquer my 2023 Goodreads challenge. It's a quick read or an even quicker listen for you speed demons out there. [2x speed gang

title: Piccadilly Jim
author: P.G. Wodehouse
review:
Piccadilly Jim is the probably the very first 20th century comedy I've ever read. I wanted a break from the high-fantasy books I usually read. And I think it perfectly served its, personally, intended purpose.

Maybe I will return to Wodehouse, or just historical-ish comedy fiction some time in the future. But I don't think it's my cup of tea for right now. While I did laugh, I was both occasionally confused and uninterested in the plot progression. It's redeeming quality was that it's a quick read; one day-er. Maybe I should try more of these, but in it's audio version.

title: A Crown of Swords
author: Robert Jordan
review: Such a refreshing change of pace. VINDICATION FOR MAT AT LAST. I love following all of their perspectives but I love Mat, and he just wasn’t getting the love he deserved, not since book 1. Gah. Also I love the Brigette dynamic. Brigette being the catalyst that “redeems” Mat was a peak satisfaction moment. Until then every chapter with Mat, Nynaeve and Elayne in it were so painful that I always put down the book for a while. Vindication. Also beautiful cliffhanger for May arc. Excited.

title: Lord of Chaos
author: Robert Jordan
review: Details. Six books and I’m drowning in details. It’s painful, but I love it. The extent of it hit me during that tavern hall scene where I could “see” the dance clearer than I could’ve had if I was actually watching one. Ugh. The damage wrought by misinformation; somethings are the same, in any world, in any setting. Misinformation. Ugh. Also, because I write reviews mostly for my future self: adoptive families scenes are the best, man, I loved it, thanks Egwene and Aiel fam. Not even halfway through. Details, details, details.

title: The Shadow Rising
author: Robert Jordan
review: For all that this series is. For how long it is. For how dense the world building is. What gets me every time is the simple, funny, brutally human misunderstanding based on male vs female perception.

title: Mistborn: The Final Empire
author: Brandon Sanderson
review: Detailed and intricate magic system and a simple yet thought provoking storyline. Especially Kelsier's arc had me hooked; his actual plan to deal with the Lord Ruler, and his fascination with the religions that survived without leaders.

title: The Wise Man's Fear
author: Patrick Rothfuss
review: It's a second book in a series of stories about a storyteller telling a chronicler the stories of his life, and it shows. The writing is extremely precise and deliberate, pregnant with mysteries and references; perfect for theories and spinoffs. It leaves you with more questions, than answers. Read at your peril, I did, knowing that the fandom has been waiting for the third and final book for nearly 10 years, with no committed release date on the horizon.

title: The Hidden Life of Trees: What They Feel, How They Communicate: Discoveries from a Secret World
author: Peter Wohlleben
review: The book that made me realise one can fall in love with non fiction books too. Truly eye opening read. Beautiful narrative that personifies the life of trees, in a way. Nobody reads this book and looks at trees, plantations and old growth forests the same again.

title: The Name of the Wind
author: Patrick Rothfuss
review: An absolutely beautiful slow-burn story that meanders from one adventure to the next. Plot ripe for speculation and full of tiny mysteries never explicitly answered. Loved it.
